reasoning:
Magnus Carlsen has maintained the FIDE world number 1 ranking continuously since July 2011. According to the November 2025 list, he retains the top rank with roughly a 23-point lead over the world's #2 player (Hikaru Nakamura/Fabiano Caruana). By November 9, he is officially #1, meaning the question effectively asks if he will remain #1 on the three subsequent resolution dates.

1. Timeline & Base Rate: 
The resolution dates are relatively close (Nov 16, 2025; Dec 9, 2025; Feb 7, 2026). The standard base rate of Carlsen remaining world #1 month-over-month is over 99%, given his 14-year streak. 
 
2. Mechanism of Losing #1: 
For Carlsen to lose his ranking, he would either need to bleed rating points through poor play, or a rival would need to gain ~24 points. Carlsen is largely inactive in classical chess at the moment, meaning his rating (around 2831-2839) is frozen. His rivals would need to actively win multiple top-level games against ~2750 opposition to cover a 23+ point gap. At the ~2800 level, gaining 23 points in a span of 1-3 months practically requires an uninterrupted, dominant super-tournament victory (scoring +5 or +6), which is exceedingly rare. 

3. Inactivity Rule:
FIDE removes players from the active list after 12 months of no classical games. Since Carlsen played in early-to-mid 2025 (e.g., Norway Chess), he won't be flagged as inactive before the final resolution date of February 2026.

With Nov 16 sharing the same monthly list as Nov 9, the probability is essentially capped at our 95% maximum. The subsequent dates see marginal but small compounding risk of a rival having a historic tournament, so uncertainty increases to 0.92 and 0.89 respectively.
